title: U.S. consumers are the “biggest losers”
summary: The Supreme Court has officially struck down the majority of President Trump’s tariffs. Scott Galloway and Jessica Tarlov discuss the implications for the U.S. economy, on Raging Moderates.
transcript_html:
YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/6faCpKKVUdU

Transcript

The Supreme Court just strike down the majority of President Trump's tariffs. >> But I think is most interesting here, Chief Justice Roberts wrote the majority opinion. But Gorsuch's concurrence, I think, is very important because it actually is talking about the importance of the legislative process and how you do actually have to go through Congress and how tempting it can be to go around them, but that it was designed this way. And I think that's the kind of push back that Americans and frankly our partners all over the world have been waiting to see for someone to say like hold on wait a minute you actually have a constitution for a reason and there are

three co-equal branches of government and enough of this doing it your own way however you want to do it. So I was heartened to see that >> I would strangely say that Trump is both a winner and a loser. This is a direct front to his authority, but at the same time, I think the markets are likely to go up based on this news. US households are short-term winners because they'll get some relief. Data showed that they were paying an incremental $1,300 per household because of elevated prices due to these tariffs. That should come down. Quite frankly, over medium and long term, US consumers are big losers

because every western nation in the world is now trying to figure out desperately how do they diversify away from us as opposed to integrate. Global trade has been massively accreative for the United States. So this will again be another in my view policy uh from Trump that future generations after he is dead and buried will continue to pay a pretty hefty price for.
